You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: The board has a white robot-like shape (top-left), a green rectangular frame (bottom-right), and red 3x3 blocks. The charcoal bar at top may be a HUD timer. The white shape appears to be the player character.
- Goal model: Position the white shape so all green pieces end up inside its hollow areas.
- Plan: Move UP to catch the green block above, then adjust position to collect all remaining green.

end of world model.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the green block that was above the white shape is now inside the white shape's top hollow area. The white shape now has green blocks in multiple hollow positions. Let me check the current state to see how much green remains.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All non-background nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:6]}")

green_total = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N')
print(f"\nTotal green pixels: {green_total}")

# Check if there are green nodes outside the white shape
white_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W']
green_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"\nWhite nodes: {len(white_nodes)}, Green nodes: {len(green_nodes)}")

# Check children (contained objects)
for wn in white_nodes:
    print(f"  White id={wn['id']} children: {wn['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so there are still 54 green pixels remaining:
1. A green block at rows 29-31, cols 41-49 (27 pixels) - this is above the white shape
2. Two green 3x3 blocks inside the white shape at rows 35-37 (18 pixels)
3. One green 3x3 block inside the white shape at rows 47-49 (9 pixels)
The green block at rows 29-31 is still above the white shape (which starts at row 32). I need to move UP more to catch it, but I also need to make sure the green blocks inside don't get lost.
Wait, actually looking at the image more carefully, there's a green block ON TOP of the white shape. Let me look at the ASCII to understand the layout better.
Actually, looking at the current image, I see:
- A green horizontal bar at the top of the white shape
- Two green squares inside the white shape's middle hollow area
- A green square at the bottom between the red blocks
The green at rows 29-31 is a 3x9 bar (27 pixels). The white shape starts at row 32. I need to move UP to catch this bar.
But wait - if I move UP, the white shape will move up and might lose the green blocks that are currently inside. Let me think about this...
Actually, the mechanic seems to be: when the white shape overlaps with green, the green parts that are covered by white pixels get absorbed, while green parts inside the hollow areas remain. So moving around should gradually collect all green pieces into the hollow areas.
Let me try moving UP more to catch the remaining green bar above.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move UP to catch the green bar above
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
